data.title: Foreign Stocks Lead Major Asset Classes In 2025
data.description:
After two straight years of performance dominance by US equities, foreign stocks in developed markets are leading the major asset classes by a wide margin so far in 2025, based on a set of ETFs through Tuesday’s close (Feb. 18). It’s anyone’s guess if the leadership for equities ex-US continues, but at the moment the change in fortunes is a striking reshuffling of recent history.

Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF (VEA) has surged 8.5% year to date, well ahead of the second-best performer, commodities (GCC), which is up 6.2%. In third place: emerging markets (VWO) with a 4.7% increase. US stocks (VTI) are currently the fourth-best performer via a 4.4% advance.

For context, the Global Market Index (GMI) is up 4.6% so far this year. GMI is an unmanaged benchmark (maintained by CapitalSpectator.com) that holds all the major asset classes (except cash) in market value weights via ETFs and represents a competitive benchmark for multi asset-class portfolios.

The changing fortunes mark a switch following two consecutive calendar years of strong leadership for US equities. In 2024, for example, American shares (VTI) rallied nearly 24%, far ahead of the rest of the field.

What’s driving the changing fortunes? Analyst Ed Yardeni this week suggests the Trump factor may be a reason:

“So far, Trump’s tariff turmoil seems to be weighing more on the US, Canada, Mexico, and many other emerging markets than on China and most European countries. That could change once reciprocal tariffs are actually imposed by the US in early April, unless Trump changes his mind, again.”

Whatever the catalyst, foreign developed markets are on a tear. VEA’s regional weighting tells the story. The Europe-developed category has the biggest weight in the fund: more than 40%, followed by Japan with a 21% share and the UK at 12%, according to Morningstar.com.

Meanwhile, the long-term expected return for US equities has fallen recently relative to the red-hot run over the past decade, based on modeling by CapitalSpectator.com. By contrast, ex ante performance for foreign-developed stocks has been revised higher lately.

Despite the revisions, US and foreign-developed stocks are currently expected to generate similar results in the 8%-9% annualized total return range over the long haul. Using this year’s rally in foreign shares as a guide, however, suggests that the crowd is pricing in a wider divergence in the near term.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TSLL Can Help Traders Juice Their Tesla Trading Performance, But Beware The Risks
data.description:
Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2X Shares ETF (NASDAQ:TSLL) is a leveraged, single-name stock ETF that aims to achieve 2x the return of the underlying Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) shares daily. This ETF was designed to provide investors with additional risk beyond directly holding TSLA shares, as well as the associated returns. Given my bullish outlook on TSLA stock, I believe actively trading TSLL can potentially add some additional positive risk to one’s trading strategy. I am rating TSLL with a BUY.

As a disclaimer, I have previously covered TSLA shares on Seeking Alpha. As a potential investor in TSLL, I highly recommend that you fully understand the various catalysts of the underlying stock before considering trading TSLL.

It should also be noted that I am bullish on TSLA shares. My rationale behind my position in Tesla is that the firm is developing a closed-loop ecosystem for autonomous robotics. Between Tesla’s Robotaxi program beginning testing in Austin, Texas, in June 2025, and California being targeted for some time in 2025, the firm is moving closer to undertaking a larger, subscription-based vehicle model. In addition to this, Tesla may be bringing its Optimus humanoid robot to the market as early as e2h26, taking on the industrials sector. Between these two initiatives, Starlink for broadband connectivity, its storage and solar panel business, and Cortex, Tesla’s AI factory, Tesla will have complete control of its technology ecosystem. You can read my most recent report here:

Tesla Will Need To Prove The Viability Of Its Robotaxi Program In June.

Looking Under The Hood Of TSLL

TSLL is a leveraged ETF, meaning that the portfolio managers actively manage a derivatives strategy to achieve the mandated goal: producing 2x the daily return on the underlying stock, TSLA. The ETF was launched on August 9, 2022, and holds an expense ratio of 96bps. TSLL has an average daily volume of 98mm shares trading hands, making this ETF a highly liquid asset for active trading.

TSLL holds various TSLA Swaps, TSLA shares, and an assortment of Treasuries that are likely held as collateral against the swap positions.

Naturally, TSLL will hold certain inherent risks as it relates to the strategy. Similar to holding your entire portfolio in a single stock, TSLL has complete exposure to TSLA shares and entirely depends on the performance of the underlying stock.

The portfolio also holds a substantial amount of leverage, meaning that the sum of the total holdings is larger than the net asset value. In this case, the total holdings of the ETF strategy amount to 309.90%. This component is necessary in order for the strategy to reach its goal, to achieve 2x the daily return of TSLA shares.

Comparing TSLA shares to TSLL, it is clear that the strategy performs very close to its intended goal.

On the other end of the spectrum, TSLL shares will result in 2x the daily loss of the underlying stock. This means if TSLA shares decline by 1%, TSLL shares will decline by 2% within a single day.

This brings forth the risk of default in the instance of extreme volatility. Though not very likely, if TSLA shares were to experience a -50% decline in a single day, TSLL shares would drop to 0. Again, though this isn’t a likely scenario, it must be considered as the worst-case-scenario that can occur in times of market stress.

Do bear in mind that this pricing chart is framed within a single day. As part of the strategy, the basis swaps reset daily, meaning that returns, or losses, can compound when TSLL is held beyond a single day. This means that multiple days of sequential gains or multiple days of sequential losses can effectively amplify gains or losses when held.

If an investor were to have held TSLL shares since inception, the investor would have experienced a loss of -19.82% rather than a gain of 25.59% when holding TSLA shares. It is clear that extending one’s holding period has the potential of compounding losses beyond realized returns. Granted, if one were to hold this strategy during an upcycle, the return profile will look entirely different.

The point that I’m trying to draw across is that due to the daily reset of the derivatives strategy, TSLL will not achieve a 2x return beyond the daily holding period. This also means that if TSLA shares were to decline by -50% over a 30-day period rather than a single day, TSLL will not reach 0 because of the daily reset. This contrasts with a single-day decline of -50% as suggested above. One interesting factor is that TSLL pays out a quarterly distribution as well as an annual short-term capital gain. Though this is a benefit to shareholders, I do not believe this feature should materially impact an investor’s decision whether to trade TSLL given that TSLL is meant to be actively traded on a daily basis and not held over time.

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in NVDL. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. The normal risks can be exaggerated with single-stock ETFs such as TSLL. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

Conclusion

TSLL is a leveraged ETF strategy that seeks to achieve 2x TSLA shares’ daily performance. This portfolio is designed to be actively traded and held for no longer than a single day. Beyond this, TSLL’s performance may be amplified beyond the 2x target, whether to the upside or downside. Given my bullish position on TSLA, I believe TSLL can provide traders a good vehicle to actively manage a position in Tesla.

data.title: Healthy Air Traffic And Improving Aftermarket Outlook Haven't Helped Melrose All That Much
data.description:
Despite a pretty constructive environment, shares of Melrose (OTCPK:MLSPF) (MRO.L) haven’t performed especially well since my last update. Up about 5%, Melrose has done okay relative to some other suppliers like Hexcel (HXL), Moog (MOG-A) (MOG-B), Thales (OTCPK:THLLY), and Woodward (WWD), but engine suppliers better leveraged to improving outlooks for deliveries from the major aircraft companies have shot ahead, with MTU Aero Engines (OTCPK:MTUAF), Rolls Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY), GE (GE), and Safran (OTCPK:SAFRY) have shot ahead with those stocks up 20% to 50% over the same time.

While longer-term comparisons are more favorable to Melrose, it’s nevertheless apparent that this isn’t a hot ticket at the moment, and I do have some concerns that investor attention will be more focused on names with more original equipment exposure in 2025 as Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) and Boeing (BA) look to be in better shape with respect to hitting production targets. I do think that Melrose remains undervalued enough to offer a worthwhile return, but this is a name that would definitely benefit from a strong beat-and-raise report when management reports full 2024 results in early March.

The Aftermarket Story Is Still Positive

Although Melrose generates the majority of its reported revenue (by a large margin) from original equipment, the profitability of the aftermarket business is considerably better and the aftermarket business is likely to grow from around 40% of profits in 2023 to around two-thirds of profits by the end of this decade. Suffice it to say, then, that this is an important part of the business.

Despite wobbly macroeconomic indicators around the world (including consumer confidence), air traffic has remained pretty healthy. IATA hasn’t yet published numbers for January air traffic, but December was up almost 9%, with full-year traffic up 10% against a roughly 4% increase in flight cycles. With flight cycles up about 5% in January, I’d expect another high single-digit to low double-digit number for traffic, and the fourth quarter reporting cycle was a strong one for U.S. airlines with multiple beat-and-raise quarters. Moreover, with global air fares tracking up 9% to 12% yoy in January, I’d say it’s not just a U.S. phenomenon.

This is good news for Melrose, as normal wear-and-tear on engines in service will eventually drive engine shop visits for the engines covered by the company’s Risk and Revenue Sharing Partnerships (or RRSPs) that account for almost half of the company’s profitability, as well as its growing repair business.

Better still, the combination of growing air traffic demand and production challenges at Airbus and Boeing are leading to lower than expected aircraft retirements, and those older aircraft in service require more aftermarket service/repair activity to keep flying. With the company actively expanding its repair business (and adding products to its repair portfolio like LEAP-1A and LEAP-1B fan blades) and present in the U.S., Europe, and Asia, this business should be able to grow from around 5% of total revenue today to closer to 10% by 2030 and with strong margins.

A Smoother Trajectory For OEM Is Positive

Whie I expect the aftermarket business to be the main driver of profit and free cash flow growth for Melrose, the original equipment business still matters – if for no other reason than it feeds future aftermarket demand and growth.

Looking back at Melrose’s third quarter update, it was clear that OE activity remains challenged, as overall Engine revenue grew 17%, but aftermarket grew 32%, while Structures revenue (which is largely OE) grew 1%. Issues with other Airbus/Boeing suppliers, particularly around areas like casting and forging, continued to be problematic, but recent reports from Airbus and Boeing (including good January deliveries) support some optimism that production is on a better, more predictable path.

With almost a decade of narrowbody and widebody business in the books for both Airbus and Boeing, I expect good growth across the next decade for Melrose. This won’t be especially profitable business upfront, but eventually these engines and aircraft will require routine maintenance and repairs, and that’s when the margin leverage really comes into play.

Visibility Remains A Challenge On Multiple Fronts

There are issues with the Melrose model that investors need to be aware of and consider.

Arguably the biggest is that the structure of the company’s RRSP leads to complicated and somewhat opaque accounting, as well as mismatches between revenue and cash flow recognition. Management has tried to be more transparent (including the offering of this “booklet” in late October), and the mismatches should shrink as engine shop visits increase over time (making the timing of FCF generation more predictable), but this has been an issue for some time and some investors don’t want to bother with companies with accounting “complications”.

There are also ongoing challenges with the profitability of the Structures business. Management has been doing the right things to improve structural profitability (consolidating sites, repricing or exiting unprofitable business, et al), but the Structures business will never be as profitable as the Engines business.

Last and not least, there are inherently unpredictable macro drivers to this business. While another global pandemic doesn’t seem particularly likely, the reality is that air travel demand is economically-sensitive and there is considerably more economic uncertainty now than a year ago. Not all of this uncertainty is necessarily bad for Melrose though – with less certainty about international security agreements, European countries could start spending more on their militaries, which would ultimately be a modest positive for Melrose.

The Outlook

Melrose seems on track for a 2024 that was basically as I expected; I’ve trimmed my revenue expectations slightly on weaker OE business, but by and large the year has developed as I expected. I likewise haven’t made major changes to my longer-term estimates, as drivers like lower aircraft retirements and increased aftermarket spending on those aircraft have been part of my thesis and model for a while.

I’m looking for around 9% revenue growth over the next five years and longer-term growth above 7% as the company benefits from expanding aftermarket demand and growing aircraft deliveries. I expect around 200-250bp of EBITDA margin improvement over the next three to five years, and I believe the maturation of the aftermarket business (and growth in the repair business) can help drive free cash flow margins toward the mid-to-high teens, driving significant free cash flow growth over the next decade.

Management has been pretty consistent about not being interested in major M&A, and so I would expect those future cash flows will eventually fund larger dividends and buybacks. I could, perhaps, see the company acquiring assets in the repair space to accelerate the growth of that business (more locations/customers or adding capabilities it doesn’t currently have), but I would be surprised if the company splashed out to add further exposure to engine components.

Between discounted cash flow and EV/EBITDA (using a 12.5x multiple, down from my prior 13.5x to reflect further maturation of the cycle), I believe Melrose shares are around 10% to 15% undervalued today.

The Bottom Line

I’m not sure 10% to 15% undervaluation is enough to make a forceful “buy” argument, particularly if there’s further rotation from aftermarket-driven names toward original equipment names. As is, I don’t dislike this company or stock, but it seems like a name that needs a meaningfully positive guidance update from management at the upcoming earnings release to renew investor interest and enthusiasm.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Consumer Spending: The Foundation Of The Bull Market
data.description:
I continue to have grave concerns about tariffs, and for reasons that have nothing to do with politics and everything to do with economics. Consumers are the backbone of our economy, and their spending is its lifeblood. Any policies that curtail spending will slow, if not undermine, the expansion and bull market. Tariffs are akin to a sales tax on the consumption of goods by consumers. The portion of the tariff that is not passed onto consumers is a tax on the business that imports the good or raw material, which increases production costs, reduces margins, and eats into profits. If there are benefits to economic growth from tariffs that are an equal offset to the loss of purchasing power, they have yet to be proven in practice. Depending on what is implemented relative to what has been proposed, I think they run the risk of cracking the foundation of our expansion and bull market.

Since the summer of 2022, as the rate of economic growth was decelerating from the sugar high of the post-pandemic period, I have asserted that the US economy would avert a recession for the simple reason that the ongoing expansion was built from the bottom up. This gave it an extremely strong foundation. Granted, the Federal Reserve did provide monetary stimulus by lowering borrowing costs to stimulate the economy from the top down, as is typical, but the unprecedented amount of fiscal stimulus that was deposited directly into the bank accounts held by lower- and middle-income households was the primary fuel for the recovery. This is because it was spent on goods and services rather than saved or invested. As we learned after the Great Recession, lowering borrowing costs to near zero does little for most consumers who can’t borrow at the depths of an economic contraction or the early stages of recovery. While the mountain of post-pandemic savings is now gone, the spending it fueled led to job creation, wage growth, and the historically low unemployment level we still have today.

So far, the widespread tariffs that were advertised on the campaign trail have not materialized, but we have seen how the threat that they may become policy dampened consumer sentiment and possibly pulled forward spending on durable goods, as evidenced by last week’s retail sales report.

At this point, existing tariffs, including the new 10% on Chinese imports, should not weigh heavily enough on consumer spending to be detrimental. While January’s retail sales declined month-over-month by the most in two years, they still rose 1.2% on an inflation-adjusted basis (real) over the past year. Compare that to the year-over-year decline of 3.2% we had in April 2023, and last month’s decline still looks healthy. Additionally, in early 2023 consumers were still focusing their spending on services over goods, and every category within the retail sales report is goods-related apart from bars and restaurants. Today, we are closer to a pre-pandemic balance.

Still, tariffs are a major concern for me, so I am extremely focused on how consumers respond, depending on what is implemented. Consumer spending will be the tip of the spear in the next inevitable economic downturn. It is likely to begin in discretionary categories first. We may receive some advanced notice in the surveys of service sector executives conducted by the Institute for Supply Management (ISM) and S&P Global, but my primary focus remains the year-over-year growth rate in real personal spending, which includes goods and services. It rose better than 3% in December, and we will have January’s number at the end of the month.

For comparison, the weakest level of real personal spending growth we have experienced since this expansion began was in November 2022 at 1.2%. That was a point in time when most economists and market strategists were forecasting a recession. It is very difficult to realize a contraction in economic activity when the largest percentage contributor to the calculation is still growing by 1.2%. Focusing on this one indicator alone kept me on the expansion track.

The sharp deceleration in the rate of spending coincided with the weakest annualized rate of economic growth we have had during this expansion at 1.3% in the fourth quarter of 2022. This was shortly after the rate of inflation peaked and the Federal Reserve raised borrowing costs to combat it. While the S&P 500 did suffer a bear market decline of 25% during the first nine months of that year, it bounced back rapidly, largely because we averted a recession. The economy has consistently expanded at a rate above its long-term trend of 2% ever since.

Consumer spending accounts for approximately two-thirds of our overall economic output, which is why the resilience of the consumer dictates the strength of the foundation of our expansion. There is an extremely high correlation between the rate of economic growth and the rate of corporate profit growth. Corporate profits are what drive stock prices.

Tariffs will weaken this foundation, but to what extent we don’t know until we understand what trade policies will be implemented. Even when we do, it will be difficult to determine how consumers respond. Last night, President Trump announced he will “probably” impose a 25% tariff on cars, drugs, and semiconductor chips as soon as April 2 with no specifics about which countries or products. Is that a negotiating tactic for better trade deals, or a targeted source of revenue for the federal government? No one knows, which makes it very difficult for companies of all sizes to conduct business. This is weighing on sentiment. I think it will continue to weigh on the rate of economic growth, as it did in last month’s retail sales report. That said, it should also be temporary until we know what policies take effect and should not disrupt the uptrend in markets or the economic expansion.

I expect focusing on the leading indicators of consumer spending will keep us on track as they did in 2022. Of course, other factors influence consumer spending, including interest rates, financial market performance, tax policies, and immigration. Some will be headwinds, while others are tailwinds that offset. The key is to consider them all in aggregate and focus on the rates of change in the data.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Out Of The Woods (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) submitted its preliminary earnings scorecard for its second fiscal quarter of FY 2025 last week after the market closed, and the server hardware company did reasonably well, causing an upward surge in the company’s share price. Super Micro Computer benefited from strong demand for its AI server products, which resulted in a double-digit increase in a company’s top line. The AI server firm also said that it expects to submit its financial reports, which have been delayed, by February 25, 2025, thereby significantly reducing its delisting risk. The company also continues to see massive revenue growth potential in the Data Center market in the next two years, and shares, given that uncertainty about a stock delisting is being reduced, appear cheap.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Super Micro Computer a buy at the end of last year because I saw a favorable demand picture developing in the market for liquid-cooled server racks that I believed was not fully reflected in the company’s stock price: A Top Rebound Play For 2025.

Super Micro Computer is also at the brink of getting back into compliance with Nasdaq by filing financial reports on time... which will prevent a stock delisting and could make shares interesting for growth investors again. With uncertainty about a stock delisting about to dissipate, and a decent outlook in place for FY 2025, I believe shares of Super Micro Computer have continual revaluation potential, and I am up-grading SMCI to strong buy.

Solid growth in Q2'25, filing issue set to be resolved by the end of the month

The server hardware company had a strong second-quarter, which was kind of expected considering that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) also reported exceptional results for its December quarter -- A Strong AI Stock For 2025 -- amid a continuing AI spending boom in the sector and escalating demand for AI chips from the Data Center industry.

AMD (AMD) also reported a significant increase in Data Center revenue, for the same reason as TSMC: chipmakers can't manufacture semiconductors fast enough given an upsurge in demand driven by technological innovation in AI.

Since Super Micro Computer reports revenue, margins and earnings on a preliminary basis right now until the auditor catches up with providing timely financial information, the company has provided estimated ranges for these metrics.

Super Micro Computer generated between $5.6-5.7B in revenue, showing 54% growth year-over-year in Q2'25. The company's adjusted earnings were between $0.58 and $0.60 per-share, which calculates to a 5% Y/Y growth rate. Margins, on both a GAAP and non-GAAP level, were reported as 11.8% to 11.9% which implies a quarter-over-quarter deterioration of 1.5 PP.

The main take-away from Super Micro Computer's (preliminary) earnings scorecard was that the company expects to regain compliance with listing rules in the last week of February:

Supermicro continues to work diligently toward the filing of its Annual Report on Form 10-K for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2024, and its Quarterly Report on Form 10-Q for the period ended September 30, 2024. Based on information currently available, the Company believes it will make such filings by February 25, 2025.

If further clarified that SMCI's audit of previous financial statements has so far not yielded major changes/restatements. Changes that were made are of minor, non-material nature and therefore don't have a major influence on my view on SMCI:

The Company is reconfirming that no previously issued financial statements require a restatement. As the Company works toward completing the audit, it made certain adjustments to the preliminary unaudited results for the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2024 that it announced on August 6, 2024. The adjustments recorded in the results for the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2024 include an increase in net sales of approximately $46 million, an increase in cost of sales of approximately $96 million, which includes a charge due to an increase in inventory reserves of approximately $45 million, and an increase in operating expenses of approximately $5 million.

New $700M convertible debt offering

In an unrelated news item, Super Micro Computer announced that it is in the process of completing a private placement of a convertible senior notes, with a 2028 maturity date, and a 2.25% coupon. The company has also said that it intends to use the $700M in expected gross proceeds for general corporate purposes as well as business expansion. While the news itself may not be that noteworthy, it is in the context of Super Micro Computer's filing delays with the Securities and Exchange Commission. Obviously, SMCI sees a chance to raise cash for investors despite its recent troubles, which further supports the idea that investors' confidence is being restored.

Outlook for FY 2025

Super Micro Computer is guiding for total revenue of $23.5-25.0B in FY 2025 which is a down-grade from an earlier range of $26.0-30.0B due to new product roll-outs, but the server company is still expected to see a solid year of business expansion, driven by an expected demand surge related to Nvidia's (NVDA) Blackwell GPUs. The new outlook implies a 62% year-over-year growth rate. For FY 2026, the company's CEO, Charles Liang, said that he sees $40B in annual revenue by as a realistic revenue target (implying 65% growth in the top line, based off of the FY 2025 mid-point of guidance).

Super Micro Computer’s valuation

Hindenburg Research’s accusations have led to a significant valuation down-turn for Super Micro Computer last year, but shares have started a fundamental recovery in November 2024… when it became more apparent that the company would be able to get back into compliance with Nasdaq and therefore could potentially avoid a devastating stock delisting. Since then, the risk matrix has improved, especially after the company found a new auditor, BDO.

Currently, Super Micro Computer is valued as a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 11.3X, which makes it one of the cheapest hardware companies with AI exposure that investors can get their hands on right now.

While Super Micro Computer has more risk than other AI/hardware companies, such as Dell Technologies (DELL) or Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), I believe the risk profile has materially improved here after the latest earnings report, especially with regard to transparency.

Dell Technologies is trading at a 10.3X P/E ratio -- based off of FY 2026 earnings -- while Hewlett Packard Enterprise, another hardware maker, is priced at 9.3X forward earnings. However, semiconductor companies like Broadcom, TSMC, Nvidia and AMD are all trading at far higher multipliers... in large part because of their strong prospects for growth related to AI applications.

Super Micro Computer, in the industry group below, has by far the strongest expected EPS growth, which relates back to SMCI having a strong focus on the server/Data Center market that is booming right now. If SMCI avoids a stock delisting, regains full NASDAQ compliance and continues to expand its business, I can see Super Micro Computer expand its valuation rate to ~15X, potentially more if investor sentiment continues to swing in SMCI's favor. A 15X P/E ratio, based off of a FY 2026 consensus estimate of $3.74 per-share, calculates to a fair value of $56.10 per-share... a price target that I believe could easily be realized by the end of the year.

Risks with Super Micro Computer

The biggest risk for Super Micro Computer relates to its bruised reputation and the restoration of investor confidence after the company's financial transparency was questioned last year. What would change my mind about Super Micro Computer is if the hardware company were to see a slowdown in its server rack business or if, against expectations, Nvidia's Blackwell ramp were to disappoint.

Final thoughts

Super Micro Computer’s earnings scorecard for the second quarter of FY 2025 has shown that investors should not yet write off the AI hardware company as it continues to benefit from very bullish fundamentals in the server market, and the company appears to be on track to put its regulatory compliance concerns into the rearview mirror. Super Micro Computer saw double-digit top line growth in the second quarter, and the company continues to expand a significant revenue boost in the next two years as Nvidia Blackwell shipments start to ramp. A restatement of financials, as I indicated in my last work on the hardware company, is highly unlikely at this point and I see potential for a continual upside revaluation if Super Micro Computer stays out of trouble and executes well in the core server market.
